value.Jurisdiction: Colorado; International; New Mexico; Oklahoma; South Dakota; Washington
value.Title: Reactivate funds solar workforce program for 50 Tribal Nations members

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHS-XPY3-RRVJ-F0DP-00000-00Reactivate funds solar workforce program for 50 Tribal Nations members2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisReactivate funds solar workforce program for 50 Tribal Nations members

Reactivate, a subsidiary of independent power producer Invenergy, is partnering with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations. Funded through Reactivates Renewable Energy Equity Foundation (REEF), this partnership will train up to 50 Tribal members through a solar curriculum, providing hands-on installation experience, industry certifications and the buildout of a dedicated solar lab to meet the rising demand for a locally supported power grid.

With an aging energy grid and the need for and an increasing need for electricity supply, the industry is experiencing a shortage of trained workers. The partnership focuses on the Tribal Workforce Development project led by TEA. Through grant funding from REEF, the program will facilitate renewable energy training across five states Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota and Washington as well as the buildout of a dedicated solar lab.

Recognizing the vital need for a clean energy workforce, Reactivate Energy Equity Foundation is excited to partner with TEA and tribal communities to support a program that produces tangible outcomes and supports Tribal members, said Utopia Hill, CEO of Reactivate.

This initiative is designed to move beyond traditional classroom instruction by providing holistic support for participants. Trainees will receive hands on solar installation experience, along with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications. The funding supports trainee stipends, resum building and job placement assistance.

The REEF Reactivate partnership is about more than funding, its about unlocking opportunity, said Tanksi Clairmont, co-executive director of TEA. By investing in the place-based tribal workforce and training model at TEA, were supporting tribal communities across five states with the skills, resource and pathways needed to lead in the clean energy economy for years to come.

value.Jurisdiction: U.S. Federal
value.Title: Colorado Energy Office Announces $12.4 Million for Geothermal Energy Projects

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ1-R213-RTBX-R1DP-00000-00Colorado Energy Office Announces $12.4 Million for Geothermal Energy Projects2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisColorado Energy Office Announces $12.4 Million for Geothermal Energy ProjectsTargeted News ServiceARLINGTON, Virginia

The National Association of State Energy Officials posted the following news:

* * *

Colorado Energy Office Announces $12.4 Million for Geothermal Energy Projects

By Jasmine Xie, NASEO

In April 2026, the Colorado Energy Office announced $12.4 million in grant funding for geothermal energy projects through the state's Geothermal Energy Grant Program (GEGP) and Geothermal Energy Tax Credit Offering (GETCO). Seven total awardees were announced, with recipients spanning schools, local governments, hospitals, and power developers for both geothermal power production and geothermal heating and cooling projects. Two projects were awarded under GEGP, enabling assessments and subsurface exploration to identify resource potential for geothermal power plants. Five projects were awarded under GETCO, including feasibility studies, detailed system designing, and direct installation of thermal energy networks and ground-source heat pumps. A press release can be found here.

With this latest round of funding, GEGP and GETCO have awarded a combined $42.6 million to geothermal energy projects across the state, advancing Governor Polis' Heat Beneath Our Feet Initiative to advance geothermal technology adoption in Western states. While this grant announcement marks the last round of grant funding available through GEGP, the GETCO tax credit program is accepting applications for its fifth round of funding until June 30, 2026, with plans to award up to $2.2 million.

Legislation is pending to provide GETCO additional funding for its future cycles due to high program demand. For information on all previously awarded projects, please visit the Colorado Energy Office Geothermal Incentive Award Tracker.

* * *

value.Jurisdiction: International
value.Title: Turkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ0-XF63-S3CF-H0SS-00000-00Turkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisTurkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities

Link to Image

Turkish entrepreneurs held meetings with delegates from various US states during this week’s SelectUSA Investment Summit, aiming to examine potential investment prospects across a wide range of industries, including renewable energy, robotics, health care technology, and dental services.



The summit took place from May 3 to May 6 in Maryland, near Washington, DC, and gathered thousands of attendees such as investors, economic development officials, and sector specialists representing more than 100 countries.

Over 30 companies from Türkiye participated in the gathering, reflecting growing interest in expanding business ties with the United States.

Tarik Sarvan, chairman of the prominent Turkish solar energy company CW Enerji, noted that the company currently exports approximately $5–$6 million worth of goods each month from Türkiye to the US and is evaluating investment possibilities on a state-by-state basis. He stated, “We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products,” emphasizing that manufacturing activities would continue in Türkiye while any US-based operations would be limited to assembly and sales.

Bakit Baydaliev, chief executive of the technology company DOF Robotics, explained that the United States already represents 40% of the company’s market share, a figure that continues to rise steadily. He added that the firm is planning to expand its immersive entertainment operations across several US states, including Montana, Wyoming, and Colorado.MENAFN09052026000045017167ID1111089585

value.Title: -Reactivate partners Tribal Energy Alternative to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHS-SBD3-SBFH-F10F-00000-00-Reactivate partners Tribal Energy Alternative to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexis-Reactivate partners Tribal Energy Alternative to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations

International Resource News - 08 May 2026

Reactivate, a US-based renewable energy organisation, has announced a new partnership with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA), a US-based organisation that supports clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations.



Funded through Reactivate's Renewable Energy Equity Foundation (REEF), this partnership will train up to 50 Tribal members through a solar curriculum, providing hands-on installation experience, industry certifications, and the buildout of a dedicated solar lab to meet the rising demand for a reliable, locally supported power grid.

The partnership focuses on the Tribal Workforce Development project led by TEA. Through grant funding from REEF, the program will facilitate renewable energy training across 5 states including Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota, and Washington-as well as the buildout of a dedicated solar lab.

This initiative is designed to move beyond traditional classroom instruction by providing holistic support for participants, and includes:

Certifications: Trainees will receive hands-on solar installation experience, along with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications.

Financial Career Support: To ensure long-term career success, the funding supports trainee stipends, resume building, and job placement assistance.

Technical Infrastructure: Funding will directly support the development of a solar lab for ongoing technical education.

[Editorial queries for this story should be sent to irn@enpublishing.co.uk ]

value.Jurisdiction: Colorado; Texas
value.Title: Houston data center to add what it claims is area's largest rooftop solar installation

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-4JB3-SCVS-M53K-00000-00Houston data center to add what it claims is area's largest rooftop solar installation2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisHouston data center to add what it claims is area's largest rooftop solar installationNaomi Klinge

A Houston data center is adding 3,150 kilowatts of rooftop solar in what it claims is the largest rooftop solar installation to date in the Houston metropolitan area.

Dallas-based DataBank is adding the solar array at its HOU3 data center located at 11003 Corporate Centre Drive in its Westway Park campus. DataBank acquired the data center in 2022 from CyrusOne Inc. (Nasdaq: CONE), along with three other sites for a total of $670 million. Altogether, the Westway Park Campus is home to three data centers.

While many massive artificial intelligence data centers currently being built require large, brand-new power generation facilities to be built on site, smaller data centers in urban areas are often just connected to the grid.

With about 6.5 MW of critical IT load at HOU3, the 3,150 kW of rooftop solar power, or 3.15 MW, could make up nearly half of the critical IT load at the facility during the day. According to DataBank's website, that data center is powered by 100% renewable electricity.

The Houston Business Journal reached out to DataBank to learn what other sources of energy power the facility, where they come from and if the facility uses battery storage but had not received a response prior to publication.

"This project shows that achieving sustainability goals directly supports strong business performance in a grid-constrained world," Ethan Eisenberg, director of Energy and Sustainability Management at DataBank, said in a news release announcing the solar array.

"By generating renewable energy on site, we're creating a cost-effective path to our net zero commitments while providing immediate value to our stakeholders."

The company said it's working to achieve net zero emissions and 100% carbon-free power by 2030. It added that HOU3 was optimal for the rooftop solar because of its large, recently constructed roof, a favorable regulatory environment and high electricity offset potential.

The solar installation will generate about 4.5 million kilowatt hours annually over an expected 25-year operational life.

Colorado-based Wunder Power will manage design, construction, monitoring and maintenance of the solar array.

After it entered the Houston market, DataBank had a presence in 27 major U.S. cities with 65 facilities and 2 million square feet of raised-floor data center capacity across its footprint.

value.Title: Top sustainable attractions in Denver for ADAPT 2026

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ1-N6W3-RRW3-N3NS-00000-00Top sustainable attractions in Denver for ADAPT 20262026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisTop sustainable attractions in Denver for ADAPT 2026

Washington, DC: U.S Green Building Council has issued the following press release:

USGBC is excited to welcome attendees from the Midwest, Southwest and Mountain regions to Denver for ADAPT 2026.

The tulips are in bloom, and spring has sprung in the Mile High City. We hope you can make time to explore some of Denver ’ s best attractions while you ’ re in town for the conference. The city is well known for its access to endless outdoor activities, top breweries, delicious food and unique landmarks such as the “Big Blue Bear” and Red Rocks Park and Amphitheatre.

The state of Colorado is also known for its environmental consciousness and sustainability leadership. The state's top sustainability achievements include

Recognition as the #1 Most Climate Resilient City in 2023 by Architectural Digest.A Top 10 State for LEED since the ranking began, moving to third in 2025.In 2024, renewable energy sources accounted for 43% of Colorado ’ s in-state electricity net generation.Stellar sustainable transportation options including the A Line train, Denver Union Station and a walkable downtown center.Voter approval of the “Waste No More” ordinance and a sales tax increase to create a Climate Protection Fund.To orient and welcome you, we ’ ve put together a few sustainability-themed activities and attractions in Denver.

Populus HotelADAPT ’ s host hotel, Populus, is the first carbon-positive hotel in the country and was certified LEED Gold in December of 2025. Populus was also named one of Time Magazine's World's Greatest Places of 2025 and Esquire Magazine's Hotel of the Year for 2025.

Red Rocks AmphitheatreThe LEED-certified Red Rocks Amphitheatre is a unique and stunning venue that balances environmental stewardship with world class entertainment. Its mission is to preserve its geological and cultural legacy for generations to come. Be sure to register for the tour, which will be offered as part of ADAPT.

Denver Botanic GardensThe Denver Botanic Gardens ’ mission to connect people with nature is properly displayed within their incredible 24-acre plot, which showcases a wide diversity of plants. Visitors can learn about plants that have adapted to the city ’ s high-altitude climate and visit gardens inspired by faraway regions across the globe.

Biking and walking trailsThe city has multiple bike parks and 850 miles of paved, off-street biking and walking trails, connecting commuters and cyclists with all parts of Denver, from downtown to the Cherry Creek Reservoir. It also has an interactive bike map to help cyclists in transit, encouraging a more environmentally friendly way to commute.

value.Jurisdiction: U.S. Federal; Colorado
value.Title: HICKENLOOPER SLAMS TRUMP'S ROLLBACK OF PUBLIC LANDS RULE

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-T7N3-SHJX-S4XF-00000-00HICKENLOOPER SLAMS TRUMP'S ROLLBACK OF PUBLIC LANDS RULE2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisHICKENLOOPER SLAMS TRUMP'S ROLLBACK OF PUBLIC LANDS RULEStates News ServiceWASHINGTON

The following information was released by Colorado Senator John Hickenlooper:

Hickenlooper: "This rollback is a blatant attack against the long-term health of our public lands."

WASHINGTON U.S. Senator John Hickenlooper released the following statement criticizing the Trump administration's decision to rescind the Bureau of Land Management's (BLM) Public Lands Rule, which made conservation an equal priority with other uses like mining and grazing on public lands.

"This rollback is a blatant attack against the long-term health of our public lands. It is a slap in the face of participatory democracy!

"The West is drying up before us, and the Trump administration wants to undo these protections anyway.

They tried to sell off public lands last year, and now they're back, stripping protections and betting we won't push back.

They're wrong. Out West, stewardship is a way of life. We'll fight tooth and nail to protect it."

Specifically, the Public Lands Rule provides the BLM with modern, science-based tools needed to protect the more than 245 million acres of public lands, including 8.3 million acres in Colorado, that the agency manages. The rule helps BLM restore degraded landscapes, conserve intact habitats, plan for responsible development, and protect cultural and natural resources.

When the Trump administration first announced plans in 2025 to rescind the rule, Hickenlooper and Representative Jared Huffman led 59 of their congressional colleagues to demand the Trump administration protect public lands and end its plans to overturn the rule. Hickenlooper supported the creation of the BLM rule in 2024 and worked with DOI and BLM to make sure stakeholders such as grazers, Tribes, and the renewables industry were included in the conversation as the rule was finalized.

The Trump administration's decision to rescind the Public Lands Rule follows their consistent efforts to undermine our public lands and national parks. Last summer Democrats defeated congressional Republicans' last-ditch effort to sell off millions of acres of public lands to pay for their tax cuts for the ultra-wealthy.

In their letter, the lawmakers highlighted the broad public support for the BLM rule when it was originally proposed. Roughly 92% of the more than 150,000 comments submitted were in support and numerous local and state officials, Tribal governments, scientists, businesses, and other stakeholders throughout the West advocated for the rule.

value.Jurisdiction: Colorado; Texas
value.Title: Both Colorado Data Center Bills Rejected in Final Days of 2026 Legislative Session Lawmakers failed to compromise after nearly a year of negotiations, intense lobbying activity and many hours of public hearings.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-TN43-S5W7-X47S-00000-00Both Colorado Data Center Bills Rejected in Final Days of 2026 Legislative Session2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisPOLITICS & GOVERNMENTBoth Colorado Data Center Bills Rejected in Final Days of 2026 Legislative SessionLawmakers failed to compromise after nearly a year of negotiations, intense lobbying activity and many hours of public hearings.Chase Woodruff

After nearly a year of negotiations, intense lobbying activity and many hours of public hearings at the state Capitol, Colorado lawmakers will end the 2026 legislative session having made no changes to how the state regulates the increasingly controversial data center industry.

Competing bills one backed by business groups, and the other by consumer and environmental advocates both failed to advance from committee in the final days of the 120-day session, and negotiations over a compromise measure didn't produce an agreement lawmakers would accept.

The compromise would have paired economic development incentives sought by data center developers with regulations on the facilities' locations, energy needs and water use, Sen. Cathy Kipp, a Fort Collins Democrat, told members of the Senate Transportation and Energy Committee Monday. With time running out before the Legislature's adjournment on Wednesday, however, she said her fellow lawmakers weren't willing to consider a so-called "strike-below" amendment that would have rewritten the entire bill.

"We really thought we were threading the needle," Kipp said. "What we came up with … was a limited, competitive tax incentive, so that if data centers are developed in Colorado, they are developed in a way that will be the most beneficial to Coloradans. But I've heard from a lot of you that a big strike-below (amendment) offered today isn't where people are."

GET MORE COVERAGE LIKE THIS

Sign up for the This Week's Top Stories newsletter to get the latest stories delivered to your inbox

Notice of Collection | Terms | Privacy

THANK YOU!

You're all set.

CLOSE

Kipp was the sponsor of Senate Bill 26-102, a measure that had the support of a coalition of Colorado's leading progressive and environmental groups. But at her request, the Transportation and Energy Committee postponed the bill indefinitely in a unanimous vote Monday. The bill included a mandate that data centers source up to 100% of their electricity from renewable energy, alongside other protections for utility customers and communities impacted by development.

House Bill 26-1030, sponsored by Rep. Alex Valdez and House Majority Leader Monica Duran, both Denver Democrats, met a similar fate last Thursday, when the House Energy and Environment Committee voted 11-2 to postpone it indefinitely. The bill centered on a 100% sales and use tax exemption for data center development, accompanied by a less stringent set of renewable energy requirements, and had the support of a wide range of tech industry groups.

Industry representatives said that such tax breaks are a prerequisite for substantial data center investment, especially when it comes to the so-called "hyperscale" facilities that serve the largest artificial-intelligence and cloud-computing companies.

"Unfortunately, we have to continue with the status quo," Valdez said before the House committee's vote. "That's not good for us, because that means Wyoming wins, and Texas wins."

HB-1030 and SB-102 were among the most heavily lobbied measures of the 2026 lawmaking session, with 196 individual lobbyists registered as staking out a position on one or both of the bills on behalf of 150 clients, according to data from the secretary of state's office.

"Nothing Gets 91%"

Amid a nationwide boom in data center construction, largely driven by investment in AI, polling shows a growing number of Americans concerned about the energy-intensive facilities' impact on the environment, and what the surging demand for energy could do to utility rates paid by consumers.

The backlash has grown more intense in recent months, as HB-1030 and SB-102 languished on the calendar at the Colorado Legislature.

Lawmakers in at least 28 of the 38 states that previously adopted sales-tax incentives for data center companies took up legislation this year to roll them back, according to an analysis by the National Conference of State Legislatures.

Kipp said legislative efforts to better regulate data centers would return next year. She pointed to the findings of a recent poll on behalf of the environmental group Conservation Colorado, which found that 91% of Coloradans support policies to "protect ratepayers, communities, and our natural resources like air and water from unrestricted data center growth."

"The people who commissioned the poll thought that they got the wrong numbers when they heard about this poll, because they're like, 'Nothing gets 91%,'" Kipp said. "That's an amazing number. And I don't think it's a stretch to say that 91% of Coloradans also don't want to write a blank check to some of the richest companies in the world, companies that have reported record profits in recent quarters."

Sen. Lisa Cutter, an Evergeen Democrat and chair of the Transportation and Energy Committee, applauded Kipp for her efforts to reach an agreement on a compromise measure, which she said industry groups should be grateful for.

"I have received so many emails about this it's bordering on the most emails of any issue I've ever received and people are overwhelmingly against any kind of incentives," she said. "The fact that you all worked so hard to put some kind of incentive structure in place is really meaningful, and I hope it's as appreciated as it should be."

"So now no one gets incentives for data centers, but we also don't have any guardrails for data centers," Cutter added. "Which is really, really troubling."

This story is republished from Colorado Newsline, a part of States Newsroom, a nonprofit news network supported by grants and a coalition of donors as a 501c(3) public charity.

value.Title: Reactivate Partners With Tribal Energy Alternative To Support Workforce Training For Tribal Members In 5 States

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-47D3-RS6P-211W-00000-00Reactivate Partners With Tribal Energy Alternative To Support Workforce Training For Tribal Members In 5 States2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisReactivate Partners With Tribal Energy Alternative To Support Workforce Training For Tribal Members In 5 States

CHICAGO, May 7, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Reactivate is proud to announce a new partnership with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations. Funded through Reactivate's Renewable Energy Equity Foundation (REEF), this partnership will train up to 50 Tribal members through a solar curriculum, providing hands-on installation experience, industry certifications, and the buildout of a dedicated solar lab to meet the rising demand for a reliable, locally supported power grid.


Continue Reading
With an aging energy grid and the need for more electricity supply due to increased demands, the industry faces a critical shortage of trained workers. The partnership focuses on the Tribal Workforce Development project led by TEA. Through grant funding from REEF, the program will facilitate renewable energy training across 5 states including Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota, and Washington-as well as the buildout of a dedicated solar lab.
"Recognizing the vital role developers play in the clean energy workforce, Reactivate is excited to partner with TEA and tribal communities to support a program that produces tangible outcomes and supports Tribal members," said Utopia Hill, CEO of Reactivate.
Program Highlights & Impact
This initiative is designed to move beyond traditional classroom instruction by providing holistic support for participants:
Comprehensive Curriculum: The program aims to graduate up to 50 trainees.Certifications: Trainees will receive hands-on solar installation experience, along with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications.Financial & Career Support: To ensure long-term career success, the funding supports trainee stipends, resume building, and job placement assistance.Technical Infrastructure: Funding will directly support the development of a solar lab for ongoing technical education."The REEF Reactivate partnership is about more than funding, it's about unlocking opportunity," said Tanksi Clairmont, Co-Executive Director of TEA. "By investing in the place-based tribal workforce and training model at TEA, we're supporting tribal communities across five states with the skills, resources, and pathways needed to lead in the clean energy economy for years to come."
About Reactivate
Reactivate, an Invenergy company, is a mission-driven organization that develops, owns, and operates renewable energy solutions that are designed to improve quality of life for communities, with a focus on meaningful benefits for working-class people across the country. Focus areas include community solar, commercial & industrial solar, small utility-scale solar, energy storage and EV charging projects.
By delivering economic development and energy resiliency with renewable energy solutions, Reactivate provides energy cost savings, job opportunities, workforce training and opportunities for businesses, while creating positive social and environmental impact. Reactivate was founded by Invenergy and Lafayette Square.
‍ About Tribal Energy Alternative (TEA)
Tribal Energy Alternatives works in partnership with Tribal communities to advance energy sovereignty and accelerate the transition to clean energy across Indian Country. Through programs like the Tribal Solar Accelerator Fund, TEA provides funding, technical assistance, and strategic support to help Tribes achieve energy self-determination and climate resilience.
SOURCE Reactivate

value.Title: Reactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 States

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-FN43-S35T-Y4F1-00000-00Reactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 States2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisReactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 States

Key Highlights:

* Reactivate partners with Tribal Energy Alternatives for clean energy workforce training in Tribal Nations.

* Program trains up to 50 Tribal members in solar installation and provides industry certifications.

* Initiative aims to graduate up to 50 trainees with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications.

Original Press Release:

Chicago, Illinois, May 7 -- Reactivate, LLC issued the following news release:

Reactivate is proud to announce a new partnership with Tribal Energy Alternatives (TEA) to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations. Funded through Reactivate's Renewable Energy Equity Foundation (REEF), this partnership will train up to 50 Tribal membersthrough a solar curriculum, providing hands-on installation experience, industry certifications, and the buildout of a dedicated solar lab to meet therising demand for a reliable, locally supported power grid.

Withan aging energy grid and the need for more electricity supply due to increased demands, the industry faces a critical shortage of trained workers. The partnership focuses on the Tribal Workforce Development project led by TEA. Through grant funding from REEF, the program will facilitate renewable energy training across 5 states including Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota, and Washington-as well as the buildout of a dedicated solar lab.

"Recognizing the vital need for a clean energy workforce, Reactivate Energy Equity Foundation is excited to partner with TEA and tribal communities to support a program that produces tangible outcomes and supports Tribal members," said Utopia Hill, CEO of Reactivate.

Program Highlights & Impact

This initiative is designed to move beyond traditional classroom instruction by providing holistic support for participants:

* Comprehensive Curriculum: The program aims to graduate up to 50 trainees.



* Certifications: Trainees will receive hands-on solar installation experience, along with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications.



* Financial & Career Support: To ensure long-term career success, the funding supports trainee stipends, resume building, and job placement assistance.



* Technical Infrastructure: Funding will directly support the development of a solar lab for ongoing technical education.

"The REEF Reactivate partnership is about more than funding, it's about unlocking opportunity," said Tanksi Clairmont, Co-Executive Director of TEA. "By investing in the place-based tribal workforce and training model at TEA, we're supporting tribal communities across five states with the skills, resources, and pathwaysneeded to lead in the clean energy economy for years to come."

About Reactivate

Reactivate, an Invenergy company, is amission-driven organization that develops, owns, and operates renewable energy solutions that are designed to improve quality of life for communities, with afocus on meaningful benefits for working-class people across the country. Focus areas include community solar, commercial & industrial solar, small utility-scale solar, energy storage and EV charging projects.

By delivering economic development and energy resiliency with renewable energy solutions, Reactivate provides energy cost savings, job opportunities,workforce training and opportunities for businesses, while creating positive social and environmental impact. Reactivate was founded by Invenergy and Lafayette Square.

About Reactivate Energy Equity Foundation (REEF)

The Reactivate Energy Equity Foundation (REEF) is 501(c)(3)non-profit organization dedicated to advancing an equitable and accessible energy transition. Formed for charitable and educational purposes, REEF focuses onsupporting workforce training and education programs within the energy sector. By providing critical funding and strategic support, the Foundation aims to build local capacity for renewable energy projects and create sustainable career pathways in the clean energy economy.

About Tribal Energy Alternative (TEA)

Tribal Energy Alternatives works in partnership with Tribal communities to advance energy sovereignty andaccelerate the transition to clean energy across Indian Country. Through programs like the Tribal Solar Accelerator Fund, TEA provides funding, technical assistance, and strategic support to help Tribes achieve energy self-determination and climate resilience.

[Category: Energy, Renewable Energy, Education, Partnerships]

Source: Reactivate, LLC

value.Title: Expanding Pathways for Thermal Energy and Geothermal Development

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH6-64W3-RX8V-H10M-00000-00Expanding Pathways for Thermal Energy and Geothermal Development2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisExpanding Pathways for Thermal Energy and Geothermal Development

The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck Mimi Larsen Julia Rhine Aaron Thiele Sloane Whelan

May 5th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

Colorado lawmakers are advancing a bipartisan proposal aimed at accelerating thermal energy resource development across the state, including geothermal energy and industrial waste. The bill reflects Colorado's broader push toward decarbonization and electrification and could potentially impact municipalities, utilities, industrial operators and energy developers.

What's In the Bill

Senate Bill 26-142, 'Concerning the Development of Thermal Energy Resources,' would significantly expand opportunities for geothermal energy development by creating clearer pathways for both distributed and utility-scale projects.

Most notably, the bill increases the allowable size of community-scale geothermal projects from 5 MW to 25 MW and contemplates that investor-owned electric utilities 'shall identify one or more potential small-scale geothermal projects' for development. At the same time, the legislation lays the groundwork for large-scale geothermal deployment by directing utilities and state agencies to identify and evaluate geothermal resources and opportunities.

SB 26-142 also contains a set of provisions that would expand the role of the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission (ECMC) and related agencies in advancing geothermal development. The bill directs ECMC, in coordination with the Colorado Geological Survey, to collect, analyze and publish data on geothermal resources statewide, including leveraging existing subsurface information such as oil and gas well data. This effort is intended to address a major barrier to geothermal investment-limited access to reliable geological data.

In addition, the legislation requires ECMC to develop recommendations to support safe and effective geothermal development and report those findings to the General Assembly by Nov. 15, 2026. The bill also directs ECMC to consider the potential creation of a geothermal development authority, which could facilitate public-private partnerships, secure federal funding and provide financial risk-mitigation tools for geothermal exploration, transmission infrastructure and thermal energy network development.

The bill passed out of the Senate on a 27-7 vote and went to the House Energy and Environment Committee, where it was amended to clarify the regulatory framework governing thermal energy networks and their interaction with existing utility laws, among other changes.

The House committee amendments confirm that local governments-and private entities acting on their behalf-may develop and operate thermal energy networks without being treated as public utilities, while expressly excluding public utilities and their affiliates from this exemption.

At the same time, the amendments preserve the rights of incumbent electric utilities by providing that utilities retain the exclusive right to serve thermal energy network facilities with retail electric service within their certificated service territories, subject to existing distributed generation rights. The amendments also clarify that nothing in the bill alters existing statutory requirements governing utility service territories or the acquisition of electric distribution infrastructure.

Finally, the amendments integrate thermal energy networks into Colorado's clean heat regulatory framework by allowing emissions reductions associated with qualifying thermal energy networks to be counted toward a utility's clean heat plan obligations, even where the utility does not own or operate the network.

All considered, SB 26-142 signals a meaningful shift: geothermal and thermal energy networks are moving from niche technologies toward more clearly defined, financeable resource classes within Colorado's broader energy portfolio. At the same time, the House committee amendments ensure that these emerging systems are integrated with rather than disruptive to the existing regulated utility framework.

Upon receiving amendments in committee, SB 142 proceeded to receive unanimous support on the House floor and will now be sent back to the Senate to concur with the amendments made in the bill's second chamber.

How It Interplays with Federal Policy Changes

At the federal level, efforts are also underway to streamline geothermal development. On April 23, 2026, the U.S. House of Representatives passed H.R. 5587, the Harnessing Energy at Thermal Sources Act of 2026 (the HEATS Act). The legislation would amend the Geothermal Steam Act of 1970 to accelerate geothermal development by reducing federal permitting and environmental review requirements for qualifying projects where existing state geothermal permitting processes are in place.

Key components of the HEATS Act:

The bill would eliminate the requirement for a federal drilling permit for geothermal exploration and production on non-federal surface lands where the United States owns less than 50% of the subsurface geothermal estate.

Eligible projects could proceed 30 days after submission of a state permit, without additional federal approvals.

Covered projects would be exempt from review under the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), the Endangered Species Act, and-unless the state lacks a historic preservation law-the National Historic Preservation Act.

Projects on Indian lands are expressly excluded and remain subject to existing federal permitting and environmental review requirements.

Federal geothermal royalty obligations and federal audit authority would remain unchanged.

If enacted, the HEATS Act would significantly reduce regulatory hurdles for many geothermal projects by shifting primary oversight to the states. This approach could materially shorten development timelines and lower costs, while making state permitting strategy an increasingly critical factor for project planning and investment decisions.

At the same time, the express exclusion of projects on Indian lands creates a bifurcated permitting regime. As a result, tribes seeking to develop geothermal resources on their lands would remain subject to a more time-consuming federal process, potentially making projects in Indian country less competitive than similar projects on state or private lands.

The HEATS Act passed the House with notable bipartisan support, including backing from a geographically diverse group of Democrats. This could lead to increased interest in the Senate to move the HEATS Act forward, including among some Democrats on the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee, who have previously been supportive of geothermal.

A separate bipartisan geothermal bill to watch is the Geo POWER Act (S. 4116), introduced by Democratic Sens. John Hickenlooper (Colorado) and Alex Padilla (California) alongside Republican Sens. John Curtis (Utah) and Steve Daines (Montana), with strong industry support and a House companion (H.R. 8437) introduced by Reps. Nick Begich (R-Alaska) and Susie Lee (D-Nevada). While the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee has struggled to move legislation in recent years, the bill could advance similarly to the HEATS and SPEED acts.

The HEATS Act is also more aggressive than prior geothermal permitting reforms considered in the 118th Congress, including the geothermal provisions in the Manchin-Barrasso Energy Reform Permitting Act, and its passage adds pressure to ongoing Senate negotiations over broader permitting reform packages spanning multiple committees.

The HEATS Act now heads to the U.S. Senate, where comprehensive permitting reform faces both procedural and political headwinds.

We will continue to monitor developments closely and are available to discuss how the HEATS Act and related permitting reform efforts at the state and federal level could affect your geothermal projects or investment strategies.

value.Jurisdiction: Arizona; Colorado; New Mexico; Utah; Washington
value.Title: Colorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy production

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JMS-VYB3-S9KK-F4G3-00000-00Colorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy production2026-05-28T15:36:55Z2026-05-28T15:36:55ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTColorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy productionRobert Tann Follow rtann@summitdailynews.com

Gov. Jared Polis said Colorado plans to be part of a multistate effort to boost geothermal energy production in the West.

Polis joined Utah Gov. Spencer Cox on Wednesday, May 20, to unveil the partnership between Colorado, Utah, Arizona and New Mexico. Speaking virtually at the event, which was held in downtown Salt Lake City, Polis said greater investment in geothermal energy will "save people money on energy bills; provide reliable, 24/7 energy; create jobs in both tech and on-site; (and) protect clean air."

"Ninety-five percent of America's capacity for geothermal is located in the western states, and it's underutilized," Polis said. "It's there, and it's ready, and we want to pursue it."

Cox said the focus of the initiative will be on building a geothermal energy workforce and attracting investment.

"If you bring Colorado on board and Arizona on board and New Mexico and all of these companies … now we've got something big that's going to attract a lot of attention and can help us move much more quickly," Cox said.

Cox also hopes that the group can serve as a conduit between the federal government and local projects when it comes to issues like permitting. Much of the land in the West is federally owned, meaning states that want to unlock more geothermal projects will likely need buy-in from the federal government. In Colorado, the federal government owns over a third of the state's land.

A multistate alliance, through its delegates in Congress, will help bolster support in Washington for geothermal projects, Cox said.

Geothermal projects grow across western Colorado

Geothermal energy is derived from heat energy that comes from the Earth's core and is pulled to the surface. A 2024 report by the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission identified three broad uses for geothermal energy.

This includes direct use that does not require energy conversion, such as hot springs, hot water and space heating. Other uses are geothermal power production, which is generated by converting thermal energy into electricity, and geoexchange, which includes using underground pipes to tap into the Earth's thermal energy to heat and cool structures above the surface.

Colorado has invested millions of dollars in recent years in geothermal projects many of which are concentrated across the Western Slope through a combination of state grant and tax credit programs.

The Aspen School District in April secured $5 million to install thermal energy infrastructure designed to heat and cool several buildings, while the town of Vail received close to $2 million in February to build out a geothermal network using sites like the public library. Other recent state funding has gone to the town of Hayden, which netted nearly $1 million to help install ground-source heat pumps across various sites, and $30,000 to help the Memorial Hospital in Craig study geothermal energy for its facilities.

As Colorado pushes more communities to wean off fossil fuels as part of its goal to reach 100% net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, some local leaders have looked to geothermal as a key part of their energy portfolio.

Vail town leaders hope investments in geothermal infrastructure will help reduce town-wide emissions by more than 10,000 metric tons annually, while officials in Hayden have floated geothermal energy as a replacement for coal to keep Hayden Station, a coal plant set for retirement, running.

Michael O'Connor, director for the newly-formed multistate group, dubbed the Mountain West Geothermal Consortium, said untapped geothermal energy could provide over 200 gigawatts of "affordable, baseload power" in western Rockies states, based on the findings of a U.S. Department of Energy study.

That represents over a quarter of current peak energy demand for the entire lower 48 states in the U.S., which is around 760 gigawatts, or 760,000 megawatts.

"It's not a commitment of any sort, but it's the size of the prize," O'Connor said during Wednesday's event in Salt Lake City.

Bill passed to incentivize more geothermal development

The multistate alliance comes on the heels of legislative action in Colorado to incentivize more geothermal energy production.

Senate Bill 142, which passed during this year's legislative session, would require investor-owned utility providers to include geothermal energy as part of their overall energy resource plan, which they submit to the Colorado Public Utilities Commission every few years. Utilities would have to solicit proposals to develop small-scale geothermal projects of up to 25 megawatts of net capacity, and large-scale projects with more than 25 megawatts of capacity.

Current law requires local governments that build or acquire gas or electric energy systems to first get voter approval. The bill passed by lawmakers removes the need for voter approval for geothermal heating and cooling systems. It would also allow local governments to issue bonds to help pay for thermal energy infrastructure.

Another provision of the bill allows industrial or commercial facilities that recover thermal energy as a by-product of their operations to sell that energy, which would not be subject to regulation by the Public Utilities Commission.

"Geothermal has been around for a long time, but it's an area that is now receiving a lot of attention," said Sen. Matt Ball, a Denver Democrat and one of the bill's lead sponsors, during the legislation's first committee hearing in April.

Ball said Colorado is well-positioned to be a leader in geothermal energy. Much of the energy production requires digging wells, jobs that can be filled from the state's oil and gas industry. Colorado also ranks as one of the top states geologically for natural geothermal resources, like hot springs.

"It's really an industry and a space that has a ton of promise, and it's one that I think we as a state should really be leaning into," Ball said.

The bill passed the Senate and House with broad bipartisan support and has been sent to Polis to be signed into law.

value.Title: Both Colorado data center bills rejected in final days of 2026 legislative session

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-47F3-S1CY-218B-00000-00Both Colorado data center bills rejected in final days of 2026 legislative session2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisNEWSBoth Colorado data center bills rejected in final days of 2026 legislative sessionChase Woodruff Colorado Newsline

After nearly a year of negotiations, intense lobbying activity and many hours of public hearings at the state Capitol, Colorado lawmakers will end the 2026 legislative session having made no changes to how the state regulates the increasingly controversial data center industry.

Competing bills one backed by business groups, and the other by consumer and environmental advocates both failed to advance from committee in the final days of the 120-day session, and negotiations over a compromise measure didn't produce an agreement lawmakers would accept.

The compromise would have paired economic development incentives sought by data center developers with regulations on the facilities' locations, energy needs and water use, Sen. Cathy Kipp, a Fort Collins Democrat, told members of the Senate Transportation and Energy Committee Monday. With time running out before the Legislature's adjournment on Wednesday, however, she said her fellow lawmakers weren't willing to consider a so-called "strike-below" amendment that would have rewritten the entire bill.

"We really thought we were threading the needle," Kipp said. "What we came up with … was a limited, competitive tax incentive, so that if data centers are developed in Colorado, they are developed in a way that will be the most beneficial to Coloradans. But I've heard from a lot of you that a big strike-below (amendment) offered today isn't where people are."

Kipp was the sponsor of Senate Bill 26-102, a measure that had the support of a coalition of Colorado's leading progressive and environmental groups. But at her request, the Transportation and Energy Committee postponed the bill indefinitely in a unanimous vote Monday. The bill included a mandate that data centers source up to 100% of their electricity from renewable energy, alongside other protections for utility customers and communities impacted by development.

House Bill 26-1030, sponsored by Rep. Alex Valdez and House Majority Leader Monica Duran, both Denver Democrats, met a similar fate last Thursday, when the House Energy and Environment Committee voted 11-2 to postpone it indefinitely. The bill centered on a 100% sales and use tax exemption for data center development, accompanied by a less stringent set of renewable energy requirements, and had the support of a wide range of tech industry groups.

Industry representatives said that such tax breaks are a prerequisite for substantial data center investment, especially when it comes to the so-called "hyperscale" facilities that serve the largest artificial-intelligence and cloud-computing companies.

"Unfortunately, we have to continue with the status quo," Valdez said before the House committee's vote. "That's not good for us, because that means Wyoming wins, and Texas wins."

HB-1030 and SB-102 were among the most heavily lobbied measures of the 2026 lawmaking session, with 196 individual lobbyists registered as staking out a position on one or both of the bills on behalf of 150 clients, according to data from the secretary of state's office.

'Nothing gets 91%'

Amid a nationwide boom in data center construction, largely driven by investment in AI, polling shows a growing number of Americans concerned about the energy-intensive facilities' impact on the environment, and what the surging demand for energy could do to utility rates paid by consumers.

The backlash has grown more intense in recent months, as HB-1030 and SB-102 languished on the calendar at the Colorado Legislature.

Lawmakers in at least 28 of the 38 states that previously adopted sales-tax incentives for data center companies took up legislation this year to roll them back, according to an analysis by the National Conference of State Legislatures.

Kipp said legislative efforts to better regulate data centers would return next year. She pointed to the findings of a recent poll on behalf of the environmental group Conservation Colorado, which found that 91% of Coloradans support policies to "protect ratepayers, communities, and our natural resources like air and water from unrestricted data center growth."

"The people who commissioned the poll thought that they got the wrong numbers when they heard about this poll, because they're like, 'Nothing gets 91%,'" Kipp said. "That's an amazing number. And I don't think it's a stretch to say that 91% of Coloradans also don't want to write a blank check to some of the richest companies in the world, companies that have reported record profits in recent quarters."

Sen. Lisa Cutter, an Evergeen Democrat and chair of the Transportation and Energy Committee, applauded Kipp for her efforts to reach an agreement on a compromise measure, which she said industry groups should be grateful for.

"I have received so many emails about this it's bordering on the most emails of any issue I've ever received and people are overwhelmingly against any kind of incentives," she said. "The fact that you all worked so hard to put some kind of incentive structure in place is really meaningful, and I hope it's as appreciated as it should be."

"So now no one gets incentives for data centers, but we also don't have any guardrails for data centers," Cutter added. "Which is really, really troubling."

value.Title: HENSEL PHELPS CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. - THE RECENT IN THE SECTOR

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-7J23-RTTV-W25P-00000-00HENSEL PHELPS CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. - THE RECENT IN THE SECTOR2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:38:29ZLexisNexisSTOCKHENSEL PHELPS CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. - THE RECENT IN THE SECTOR

DASHBOARD: HENSEL PHELPS CONSTRUCTION CO

PermID4296577863Websitehttp://www.henselphelps.comIndustryConstruction

ACTIVITIES:

Hensel Phelps Construction Co., Inc., founded in 1937, is an employee-owned general contractor headquartered in Greeley, Colorado. With over 4,000 employees across 10 U.S. offices, it delivers construction and renovation projects in aviation, commercial, healthcare, education, and government sectors. Generating over $6 billion annually, notable projects include the Pentagon renovation and San Jose International Airport modernization. In 2024, Hensel Phelps advanced its use of virtual design and construction (VDC) technologies, with VDC Manager Mark Blacklin winning a drone challenge for high-quality imaging. Its culture emphasizes safety, collaboration, and innovation, fostering a commitment to excellence through employee ownership. Competing with Turner Construction, Hensel Phelps stands out for its integrated project delivery and sustainable practices. Recent initiatives include expanding renewable energy projects and enhancing safety programs like Construction Safety Week.

+ HENSEL PHELPS CONSTRUCTION CO [3 News That Matters]

+ TRACK3D SECURES MAJOR PARTNERSHIP WITH HENSEL PHELPS FOR CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY

Track3D, an AI-driven construction technology company, has marked a significant milestone by securing a multi-year agreement with Hensel Phelps, expanding its collaboration from a single pilot to the deployment of Track3D’s Reality Intelligence platform across more than 200 active projects. This announcement was made on 1st April 2026, emphasizing Track3D's growing influence in the construction sector.Hensel Phelps plans to standardize the use of Track3D for portfolio-wide progress monitoring, indicating strong confidence in the platform’s scalability and effectiveness across diverse construction projects. By utilizing automated progress tracking, Hensel Phelps aims to identify on-site issues earlier, which can significantly reduce delays and enhance schedule reliability, especially in complex builds.A notable case study from San Francisco International Airport highlighted the successful application of Track3D’s technology on a challenging infrastructure project. Project leaders reported a shift from reactive issue resolution to proactive decision-making, facilitated by 360deg site walks and the involvement of existing field personnel, thereby minimizing the need for major workflow changes. This deployment at SFO reinforces Track3D's relevance in high-value infrastructure work and solidifies its position in the reality intelligence market.In addition to the partnership with Hensel Phelps, Track3D has introduced a March update to its platform that focuses on large-enterprise oversight and workflow efficiency. Key enhancements include an admin dashboard and Groups functionality, which are designed to provide major contractors with centralized portfolio visibility and streamlined user management. Furthermore, the new capture-free progress tracking feature allows teams to document job site status directly on floorplans, promoting continuous oversight.Track3D is also intensifying its go-to-market strategy by targeting specialty contractors, with plans to participate in the AWCI Convention & Expo 2026. The company aims to showcase tools that automate production tracking and reduce manual reporting for drywall and ceiling professionals. Collectively, these developments—ranging from the Hensel Phelps portfolio rollout to enterprise-focused product enhancements—underscore Track3D’s strengthening competitive position in construction technology and may pave the way for sustainable growth in the future.

+ MICAH CHOY STEPS UP AS PROJECT SUPERINTENDENT AT HENSEL PHELPS

Micah (Pono) Choy has been promoted to project superintendent at Hensel Phelps, overseeing the Hawaii Department of Education’s Regional Kitchen – North Central Oahu project in Wahiawa. As of 1st April 2026, Choy's promotion marks a significant milestone in his career with the company, which he joined in 2018.During his tenure at Hensel Phelps, Choy has played a pivotal role in various major projects across the aviation, wastewater, and federal market sectors in Hawaii and Guam. His collaborative leadership style has garnered recognition, allowing him to build strong partnerships across teams and effectively drive project outcomes.Choy's promotion to project superintendent reflects Hensel Phelps' commitment to developing talent within the organization. His experience and expertise will be instrumental in managing the Regional Kitchen project, which is critical for enhancing educational facilities in Hawaii.The construction industry continues to evolve, and Hensel Phelps is positioning itself for future growth by promoting leaders like Choy. His ability to navigate complex projects while fostering teamwork aligns with the company's strategic goals.As Hensel Phelps embarks on new initiatives, Choy's leadership will likely contribute to the successful execution of high-profile projects. Stakeholders and investors will be watching closely as the company continues to build its reputation in the construction sector.

+ TRACK3D PARTNERS WITH HENSEL PHELPS FOR MAJOR CONSTRUCTION ROLLOUT

Track3D, an AI-driven construction technology company, has announced a significant milestone in its growth trajectory as it secures a multi-year enterprise agreement with Hensel Phelps. As of 25th March 2026, this collaboration expands from a single pilot project to deployment across more than 200 active projects, marking a notable shift in how the construction industry embraces advanced technology.Hensel Phelps plans to standardize Track3D’s Reality Intelligence platform portfolio-wide for progress monitoring, demonstrating a strong confidence in the technology's scalability across diverse project types. This extensive rollout is expected to deepen recurring revenue streams for Track3D, enhance customer loyalty, and establish a high-profile reference account that could attract additional large contractor deals.In addition to the enterprise agreement, Track3D highlighted a successful case study at San Francisco International Airport. Hensel Phelps utilized Track3D's technology on this demanding build, where project leaders noted that the platform facilitated a shift from reactive to proactive decision-making. The deployment at SFO showcases how Track3D can enhance progress tracking and coordination across trades without necessitating significant changes to existing workflows.On the product development front, Track3D has announced a March release aimed at improving enterprise oversight and workflow efficiency. New features include an admin dashboard and Groups functionality, which allow for centralized portfolio visibility and streamlined user management. Furthermore, enhancements such as capture-free progress tracking capabilities enable teams to document job site status directly on floorplans, marking a significant improvement in operational flexibility for enterprise users.Track3D is also advancing its go-to-market strategy by focusing on specialty contractors, with plans to participate in the AWCI Convention & Expo 2026. This event is pivotal for drywall and ceiling professionals, and Track3D aims to showcase tools that automate production tracking and enhance margin protection. Overall, the recent developments with Hensel Phelps, the SFO case study, and the March product upgrades indicate a period of meaningful commercial traction for Track3D, potentially solidifying its competitive position in the construction technology landscape.

+ KIEWIT CORPORATION [3 News That Matters]

+ KIEWIT CORPORATION PARTNERS IN $7.1 BILLION AUSTIN LIGHT RAIL PROJECT

A joint venture between Kiewit Building Group and Austin Commercial has secured a significant design-build contract to construct an operations and maintenance facility for the US$7.1 billion Austin Light Rail line. This announcement was made on 28th April 2026, marking a pivotal moment in the development of public transit infrastructure in Austin, Texas.The planned railway system will encompass 15 stations along a 10-mile stretch, featuring all-electric trains expected to run every five to ten minutes throughout most of the day. Kiewit Corporation, in collaboration with local partner Austin Commercial, will oversee the design and construction of the facility where Austin’s light rail vehicles will be stored, serviced, and dispatched, ensuring operational efficiency.In addition to storage and maintenance, the facility will provide a workspace for operators, maintenance crews, and support staff, thereby contributing to the overall functionality of the light rail system. The selected team for this project includes 10 subcontractors focused on the design phase, which is anticipated to generate thousands of jobs during both the design and construction phases.Greg Canally, CEO of the Austin Transit Partnership (ATP), emphasized the importance of this development, stating, "This is another major step forward, and we now have the full construction team that will deliver Austin’s light rail system. The Operations and Maintenance Facility is integral to the success of the system—it’s where trains are maintained, cleaned and prepared for service each day." Canally highlighted that partnering with a world-class construction firm like Kiewit Corporation, which possesses deep local experience, positions the project for long-term success while fostering job creation and economic opportunities across Central Texas.Previously, an Aecom-led consortium named LINC Austin was awarded the role of delivery partner for the first phase of the larger rail project in December 2024. This consortium includes notable firms such as Parsons, STV, Turner & Townsend, Ardmore Roderick, and CAS Consulting, all of which will play a crucial role in the realization of Austin's light rail ambitions.

+ KIEWIT CORPORATION SELECTED FOR EAST AUSTIN LIGHT RAIL PROJECT

A major piece of Austin's long-discussed light rail is finally moving from plans to prep work. On Wednesday, the Austin Transit Partnership signed off on Kiewit Austin Partnership as the design-build contractor for the light rail Operations and Maintenance Facility, clearing the way for pre-construction and inching a central Project Connect component closer to groundbreakers and bulldozers near the airport. The date of this significant development is 21st April 2026.According to Austin Transit Partnership, the ATP board approved Kiewit Austin Partnership—a joint venture of Kiewit Building Group Inc. and Austin Commercial—as the design-build contractor for the Operations and Maintenance Facility. ATP CEO Greg Canally remarked, “This is another major step forward, and we now have the full construction team that will deliver Austin’s light rail system.” The agency's decision allows KAP to immediately launch pre-construction work, including advancing design, starting the permitting process, and preparing the site for future construction.The operations and maintenance facility is slated for a 62-acre site at the southwest edge of the Montopolis neighborhood, according to ATP. The planned footprint, near U.S. Highway 183 and State Highway 71, will house storage and servicing for up to 40 light rail vehicles and will necessitate property acquisitions affecting dozens of existing businesses. Local reporting has highlighted that roughly one-third of the businesses expected to be displaced are concentrated in Airport Commerce Park, raising concerns about relocation and neighborhood impacts.With KAP in place, ATP plans to expedite the Operations and Maintenance Facility design through permitting and site preparation, paving the way for the broader light rail project to advance toward full construction. Industry estimates indicate that mainline construction could commence in 2027, with initial service targeted for the early 2030s. The Federal Transit Administration's combined Final Environmental Impact Statement and Record of Decision, published in January 2026, has cleared the project to enter final design and begin land acquisition.ATP noted that the KAP team already includes around 10 subcontractors during the design phase, with opportunities expected to increase once construction ramps up. The larger Project Connect program is projected to create or support thousands—potentially tens of thousands—of jobs across Texas during design and construction. On the operations side, the maintenance facility is expected to support approximately 300 long-term jobs once the system is operational, further contributing to local economic growth.

+ ATERNIUM ENGAGES KIEWIT FOR HYDROGEN PRODUCTION FACILITY PRE-FEED

Aternium has appointed Kiewit Engineering Group Inc. to undertake the pre-front-end engineering and design (pre-FEED) for its inaugural integrated hydrogen and heavy water production facility. This strategic move comes as Aternium aims to advance its ambitious $1 billion plan to produce clean hydrogen and critical heavy water domestically. The announcement was made on 26th March 2026, signaling a significant step in the company's broader, multi-plant development strategy focused on establishing a robust infrastructure for advanced energy materials.The planned facility will be located in the Mid-Atlantic region of the United States, designed to meet the growing demand for low-carbon energy solutions. Aternium's initiative not only seeks to enhance domestic capabilities in the production of heavy water—currently sourced primarily from countries like China and Iran—but also aims to address critical supply chain vulnerabilities. Andrew Cottone, Founder and CEO of Aternium, emphasized the importance of this collaboration with Kiewit, citing shared values in technical expertise and safety standards.According to Cottone, the initiative is poised to create significant socioeconomic benefits by fostering workforce development and job creation in local communities. Aternium's focus on integrating advanced technologies and innovative processes is expected to stimulate regional economic growth, thereby supporting the transition to cleaner energy systems. Kiewit will play a pivotal role in executing Aternium's unique business model, which aims to deliver both clean hydrogen and heavy water at competitive costs.Kiewit’s involvement in the project brings extensive experience in engineering, procurement, and construction within the energy sector. Tyler Nordquist, Executive Vice President at Kiewit Engineering Group Inc., expressed confidence in the partnership, highlighting the company's ability to add substantial value during the pre-FEED phase. This phase will include defining the project's scope, optimizing design parameters, and laying a solid foundation for future development.Aternium's integrated production approach is noteworthy as it combines clean hydrogen generation with a patent-pending process for isolating heavy water. This dual-output model not only aims to reduce hydrogen production costs but also addresses a critical vulnerability in the U.S. supply of strategic materials. With the increasing demand for clean energy and high-tech components, Aternium's initiative is strategically positioned at the intersection of energy transition and advanced manufacturing, thus offering a scalable solution that aligns with both economic and technological priorities.

+ TURNER CONSTRUCTION [3 News That Matters]

+ TURNER CONSTRUCTION UNVEILS FREE AI TOOL FOR ENHANCED JOBSITE SAFETY

Turner Construction has launched SafeT Coach, a free AI-powered tool designed to enhance safety on construction job sites. This innovative solution is accessible from any mobile device and aims to facilitate real-time discussions centered around safety protocols. On 9th May 2026, the company highlighted that SafeT Coach answers safety-related questions using Turner’s Environmental Health & Safety standards, rather than generic sources available on the internet.The tool is engineered to foster a coaching mindset among supervisors and craft professionals, focusing on dialogue about safer work methods rather than mere enforcement of rules. Outputs generated by SafeT Coach are framed as potential hazards, supporting the professional judgment of users. The development of this tool involved collaboration with over 80 stakeholders through design exercises and field pilots, ensuring its relevance and effectiveness.Since its pilot introduction, SafeT Coach has recorded more than 25,000 interactions involving Turner staff, trade partners, and field teams. For instance, Darren Dreas, a superintendent working on a higher-education lab project, utilized the tool to evaluate whether a vertical shaft met the criteria for a permit-required confined space. With SafeT Coach, he was able to quickly access a decision flow chart, a start-of-day permit checklist, and relevant policy citations, facilitating an informed discussion with a trade partner’s safety manager.Dreas commented on the efficiency of the tool, stating, “Using SafeT Coach saved hours of research and analysis, helping us to quickly make informed decisions.” This positive feedback underscores the potential impact of SafeT Coach on improving safety protocols in the construction industry, enabling better decision-making on-site and promoting a culture of safety.As construction continues to evolve with technology integration, Turner Construction's introduction of SafeT Coach represents a significant step towards leveraging AI for practical safety solutions. The tool's free accessibility opens opportunities for various companies across the industry to enhance their safety measures without incurring additional costs, potentially leading to broader adoption of advanced safety practices.

+ TURNER CONSTRUCTION SELECTED FOR MAJOR $500 MILLION WESTCOURT PROJECT

Turner Construction Company has been selected to serve as the general contractor for the ambitious Westcourt project, a $500 million mixed-use development located adjacent to the Kia Center in downtown Orlando. This announcement was made on 5th May 2026, marking a significant step forward for a project that has faced multiple delays in recent years.The Westcourt development is planned to cover approximately 8.5 acres and will feature a sports and entertainment district ranging from 900,000 to 1 million square feet. Key components of the project include a 265-key Kimpton-branded hotel, 269 high-rise residential units, and up to 310,000 square feet of Class A office space. Additionally, the plan encompasses over 125,000 square feet dedicated to dining and entertainment, a 3,500-capacity venue for live events, and 1,140 on-site parking spaces.Turner Construction has indicated that during peak construction periods, the project will support around 800 workers on-site and is expected to create approximately 1,600 direct construction jobs throughout its duration. The firm anticipates engaging many trade partners from the Central Florida area, which could bolster local employment opportunities.In April 2024, the project received necessary land use entitlements, with the Kimpton hotel partnership announced later that same year, marking the brand's inaugural location in Orlando. Public support has also been secured, with Orlando’s Community Redevelopment Agency committing $2.5 million to the project, potentially reimbursing the developer up to $40 million over time through future property tax growth generated by Westcourt.Looking ahead, development documents suggest a targeted groundbreaking in the second quarter of 2026, with the projected opening set for late 2028. While construction cranes are not expected to rise immediately, the attachment of a reputable builder like Turner Construction brings renewed momentum to one of downtown Orlando's most anticipated projects.

+ TURNER CONSTRUCTION EXPANDS INDIAN OPERATIONS WITH NEW WEWORK WORKSPACE

Turner Construction is solidifying its presence in India with the establishment of a new managed workspace in Airoli, Mumbai. This expansion marks a significant milestone for the global construction firm as it partners with WeWork India. The new facility, which spans 46,000 square feet and accommodates over 650 professionals, was opened on the 7th of May, 2026, reflecting Turner’s long-term commitment to India's infrastructure and talent ecosystem.The decision by Turner Construction to consolidate its teams in a large-scale managed office underscores the growing recognition among enterprises that workspace design can be a strategic enabler of collaboration and operational agility. Exclusively designed for Turner, this office aligns with the company's global standards while leveraging WeWork India's design capabilities. Jairam Panch, Chief Operating Officer of Turner Construction, stated that the new workspace is more than just a physical location; it embodies the company's investment in an environment that fosters collaboration, creativity, and excellence.Turner’s relationship with WeWork India began in 2022 with a modest 16-seat private office, which has now evolved into a comprehensive enterprise-grade managed office solution. As Turner's operations in India expanded, the need for a flexible workspace that supports employee convenience and corporate identity became paramount. Airoli was chosen for its connectivity, talent access, and burgeoning business ecosystem. The managed office incorporates Turner’s precision-focused approach along with WeWork India's design thinking, featuring collaborative spaces and modern amenities.The rise in demand for managed offices like the one established by Turner Construction reflects a broader shift in enterprise perspectives on workspaces. As noted by Santosh Martin, Chief Revenue Officer of WeWork India, companies are increasingly viewing their work environments not just as operational assets, but as critical drivers of organizational performance and culture. Turner’s transition from a small office to a larger, customized workspace exemplifies the trend towards flexible models that facilitate growth while maintaining a strong corporate identity.With this latest addition, WeWork India continues to expand its footprint among enterprise clients, with 76 percent of its member base in Mumbai representing various sectors, including technology and finance. Operating across eight cities with over 100,000 desks, WeWork India is positioning flexible workspaces as an essential component of modern business infrastructure in the country.
